{"parentUuid":null,"isSidechain":true,"promptId":"1cc8c58e-5118-404c-b220-d25731c48877","agentId":"a7ccd0aa06f9ce80b","type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":"You are a historical research assistant. Do heavy web research on ALL major territorial/border changes from 1920 to 1991. This is for an academic project tracking \"polities\" (political entities defined by territory over time).\n\nFor each event, provide: exact year, territories involved, nature of change, area affected (km²) if possible, and resulting new polities.\n\nCover EXHAUSTIVELY:\n1. **Interwar period (1920-1938)**: Treaty of Trianon consequences (Hungary loses territory), Vilnius dispute Lithuania/Poland, Memel/Klaipeda, Free City of Danzig, Saar Basin, Italian acquisitions (Dodecanese, Istria), Irish Free State 1921/1922, Saudi Arabia unification 1924-1932, Manchukuo 1932\n2. **WWII territorial changes (1938-1945)**: Anschluss (Austria annexed 1938), Munich Agreement (Sudetenland), Hungary gains from Vienna Awards 1938/1940, USSR annexes Baltic states 1940, Bessarabia/Bukovina 1940, Finland loses Karelia 1940, Bulgaria gains Southern Dobruja 1940, Italy invades Ethiopia 1936, Japanese conquests in Asia\n3. **Post-WWII (1945-1950)**: Germany split into occupation zones then two states (1949), Poland border shifts (Oder-Neisse line), USSR keeps Baltic states/eastern Poland/Bessarabia/Karelia/Königsberg, Trieste dispute, Korean split 1945/1948, India/Pakistan partition 1947, Israel creation 1948, Indonesia independence 1945-49, China becomes PRC 1949\n4. **Decolonization Africa (1956-1968)**: Sudan 1956, Ghana 1957, Guinea 1958, massive wave 1960 (17 countries!), Algeria 1962, Kenya 1963, Malawi/Zambia 1964, Gambia 1965, Botswana/Lesotho 1966, Equatorial Guinea 1968, etc. Give EVERY country and year.\n5. **Decolonization Asia/Middle East**: Malaya/Malaysia formation 1957/1963/Singapore separation 1965, Cambodia 1953, Laos 1954, Vietnam split 1954, Kuwait 1961, Aden/South Yemen 1967, Bahrain/Qatar/UAE 1971, Brunei 1984\n6. **Decolonization Americas/Oceania**: Jamaica/Trinidad 1962, Guyana 1966, Barbados 1966, Fiji 1970, PNG 1975, Suriname 1975, etc.\n7. **Cold War era changes**: Sino-Indian War 1962, Six-Day War 1967 (Israel occupies Sinai, Golan, West Bank, Gaza), Yom Kippur 1973, Camp David 1978 (Sinai returned 1982), Vietnam reunification 1975, Western Sahara/Mauritania/Morocco 1975-1979, Indonesia annexes East Timor 1976, China-Vietnam War 1979\n8. **USSR dissolution 1991**: 15 new states - list them ALL with dates\n\nFor each change give the EXACT year and territories. Be exhaustive."},"uuid":"75e8ce62-70c2-4b63-944d-95d6d52c7b78","timestamp":"2026-03-24T20:15:17.173Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
{"parentUuid":"75e8ce62-70c2-4b63-944d-95d6d52c7b78","isSidechain":true,"agentId":"a7ccd0aa06f9ce80b","message":{"model":"claude-opus-4-6","id":"msg_01XFRv1WoMKrt8whdsNYqD7Y","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"text","text":"\n\n# Comprehensive Catalog of Major Territorial/Border Changes, 1920–1991\n\nThis is an exhaustive reference for tracking polities (political entities defined by territory over time). Each entry provides the year, territories involved, nature of change, area affected where available, and resulting new polities.\n\n---\n\n## 1. INTERWAR PERIOD (1920–1938)\n\n### Treaty Settlements and Immediate Aftermath (1919–1923)\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Trianon (signed June 4, 1920; effective July 31, 1921)**\nHungary loses approximately 72% of its pre-war territory (325,411 km² → 93,073 km²):\n- **Transylvania and eastern Hungary** → Romania (~103,000 km², including Transylvania proper, Crișana, Maramureș, and part of Banat)\n- **Slovakia and Ruthenia (Subcarpathian Rus)** → Czechoslovakia (~63,000 km²)\n- **Croatia-Slavonia, Vojvodina, Prekmurje, and western Banat** → Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes (Yugoslavia) (~63,000 km²)\n- **Burgenland** → Austria (~4,000 km²; formally transferred 1921)\n- **City of Fiume (Rijeka)** → disputed; Free State of Fiume declared (1920–1924)\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Sèvres (August 10, 1920)** (largely superseded by Lausanne 1923)\nPartitioned the Ottoman Empire:\n- **Thrace (eastern)** → Greece (later returned to Turkey at Lausanne)\n- **Smyrna/Izmir zone** → Greek administration (reversed by Turkish War of Independence)\n- **Armenia** → recognized as independent (Treaty Armenia, ~160,000 km² on paper; never implemented)\n- **Kurdistan** → autonomy provisions (never implemented)\n- **Mesopotamia (Iraq)** → British Mandate (~435,000 km²)\n- **Palestine** → British Mandate (~26,000 km², later split with Transjordan)\n- **Transjordan** → separated from Palestine Mandate 1921 (~89,000 km²)\n- **Syria and Lebanon** → French Mandate (~185,000 km² combined)\n- **Dodecanese Islands** → confirmed to Italy (~2,714 km²; held since 1912)\n- **Hejaz** → recognized independent\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Tartu (February 2, 1920)**\nSoviet Russia recognizes **Estonia**'s independence. Estonia retains Petseri/Pechorsky district and trans-Narva territories (~2,300 km² beyond later Soviet borders).\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Tartu (October 14, 1920)** (separate treaty, same name)\nSoviet Russia recognizes **Finland**'s independence with borders including **Petsamo/Pechenga corridor** giving Finland access to the Arctic Ocean (~10,470 km²).\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Riga (August 11, 1920)**\nSoviet Russia recognizes **Latvia**'s independence with borders including Latgale.\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Moscow (July 12, 1920)**\nSoviet Russia recognizes **Lithuania**'s independence, including recognition of Lithuanian sovereignty over Vilnius/Wilno.\n\n**1920 – Vilnius Dispute**\nOctober 1920: Polish General Żeligowski seizes **Vilnius (Wilno)** and surrounding region from Lithuania (~17,000 km²). Establishes puppet \"Republic of Central Lithuania.\" Formally annexed by Poland in 1922. Lithuania never recognized this; considered itself in a state of war with Poland; made **Kaunas** its temporary capital. Lithuania did not regain Vilnius until 1939 (Soviet intervention).\n\n**1920 – Free City of Danzig established (November 15, 1920)**\nUnder League of Nations protection per Treaty of Versailles. Area: ~1,966 km². Population predominantly German. Poland given special rights (postal service, railway, foreign representation). Existed as a quasi-independent polity until Nazi annexation September 1, 1939.\n\n**1920 – Saar Basin Territory established**\nUnder League of Nations administration per Versailles Treaty. Area: ~1,912 km². Coal mines given to France as reparation. Plebiscite scheduled for 15 years hence. (Returned to Germany by plebiscite January 13, 1935, effective March 1, 1935.)\n\n**1920 – Klagenfurt Plebiscite (October 10, 1920)**\nSouthern **Carinthia** votes to remain with Austria rather than join the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats, and Slovenes. ~2,920 km² remains Austrian.\n\n**1920 – Schleswig Plebiscites (February–March 1920)**\n**Northern Schleswig (Zone 1)** votes to join Denmark (75% for Denmark). ~3,984 km² transferred. **Southern Schleswig (Zone 2)** votes to remain German (80% for Germany).\n\n**1920 – Treaty of Rapallo (November 12, 1920)** (Italy-Yugoslavia)\n- **Istria** → Italy (~4,955 km² of Istrian Peninsula plus Zadar/Zara enclave)\n- **Fiume/Rijeka** → Free State of Fiume (1920–1924); annexed by Italy 1924 via Treaty of Rome\n\n**1921 – Irish Free State**\nAnglo-Irish Treaty signed December 6, 1921. The **Irish Free State** formally established December 6, 1922, as a Dominion. Territory: 26 of 32 Irish counties (~70,273 km²). **Northern Ireland** (6 counties, ~14,130 km²) remains in the United Kingdom. Boundary Commission (1924–1925) made only minor adjustments; border essentially as originally drawn.\n\n**1921 – Åland Islands**\nLeague of Nations rules in favor of Finnish sovereignty over the **Åland Islands** (June 1921), rejecting Sweden's claim. Area: ~1,580 km². Demilitarized and autonomous status guaranteed.\n\n**1921 – Treaty of Riga (March 18, 1921)** (Poland-Soviet Russia)\nEnds Polish-Soviet War. **Eastern Poland (Kresy)** — territories of western Belarus and western Ukraine — retained by Poland. Poland's eastern border set well east of the Curzon Line. Area gained by Poland: ~135,000 km² (Western Belarus ~108,000 km² and Western Ukraine portions). This border held until 1939.\n\n**1921 – Upper Silesia Partition**\nFollowing plebiscite (March 20, 1921) and Third Silesian Uprising (May–July 1921), the League of Nations divides Upper Silesia (October 1921, effective June 1922):\n- Eastern portion (~3,213 km², containing most industrial areas) → Poland (as Silesian Voivodeship)\n- Larger western portion (~9,700 km²) → Germany\n\n**1922 – Egypt nominal independence (February 28, 1922)**\nBritain unilaterally declares Egyptian independence but retains control over defense, foreign affairs, Sudan, and Suez Canal. Egypt ~1,001,450 km². (Full sovereignty not achieved until 1936 treaty, and British military withdrawal not complete until 1956.)\n\n**1923 – Treaty of Lausanne (July 24, 1923)**\nSupersedes Treaty of Sèvres. Establishes borders of the **Republic of Turkey** (declared October 29, 1923):\n- Turkey recovers **Eastern Thrace** from Greece (to Maritsa/Evros River, ~23,764 km²)\n- Turkey recovers **Smyrna/Izmir** and all of Anatolia\n- Turkey retains **Kurdistan** region (no autonomy)\n- **Hatay/Alexandretta** remains part of French Syria (transferred to Turkey in 1939)\n- Confirms cession of Arab provinces, Dodecanese (to Italy), Cyprus (to Britain)\n- Greco-Turkish population exchange (1923–1924): ~1.5 million Greeks from Turkey and ~500,000 Turks from Greece\n\n**1923 – Memel/Klaipėda (January 15, 1923)**\nLithuania seizes the **Memel Territory/Klaipėda Region** (~2,848 km²) from French-administered League of Nations mandate. Conference of Ambassadors recognizes Lithuanian sovereignty in 1924 with autonomous status for the territory. (Germany reclaims it March 22, 1939.)\n\n**1924 – Treaty of Rome (January 27, 1924)**\nItaly annexes **Free State of Fiume/Rijeka** (~28 km² city). Yugoslavia receives nearby Porto Baros and delta.\n\n### Arabian Peninsula Consolidation\n\n**1921 – British Mandates formalized**\n- **Iraq**: British Mandate (League of Nations, 1920; Kingdom established 1921 under Faisal I). ~435,052 km².\n- **Palestine**: British Mandate (~26,625 km² west of Jordan River)\n- **Transjordan**: Separated from Palestine Mandate, April 1921, as Emirate under Abdullah I (~89,342 km²)\n\n**1924 – Hejaz conquered by Ibn Saud**\nThe **Sultanate of Nejd** (Ibn Saud) conquers the **Kingdom of Hejaz** (including Mecca and Medina). October 1924 Mecca falls. Kingdom of Hejaz and Nejd proclaimed January 8, 1926.\n\n**1932 – Kingdom of Saudi Arabia proclaimed (September 23, 1932)**\nUnification of **Hejaz, Nejd, Al-Hasa, and Asir** into the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. ~2,149,690 km².\n\n**1932 – Kingdom of Iraq independence (October 3, 1932)**\nBritish Mandate ends. Iraq admitted to League of Nations as sovereign state. ~435,052 km².\n\n### Other Interwar Changes\n\n**1924 – Mongolia**\n**Mongolian People's Republic** established November 26, 1924 (Soviet satellite). ~1,564,116 km². Formerly Bogd Khanate (de facto independent from China since 1911/1921).\n\n**1926 – Lebanon**\n**Lebanese Republic** proclaimed May 23, 1926, under French Mandate (Greater Lebanon since 1920). ~10,452 km².\n\n**1931 – Statute of Westminster (December 11, 1931)**\nGrants full legislative independence to Dominions: **Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, Irish Free State, Newfoundland**. These become fully sovereign polities (though some, like Australia, didn't adopt the statute until later).\n\n**1932 – Manchukuo (March 1, 1932)**\nJapan establishes puppet state of **Manchukuo** in Manchuria (northeast China). ~1,192,000 km². Nominally headed by Puyi (last Qing emperor). Expanded 1933 to include Jehol province (~170,000 km² added). Dissolved August 1945 with Japanese surrender; territory returned to China.\n\n**1934 – Merging of Hejaz and Nejd governance**\nCompleted under Saudi Arabia (see 1932 above).\n\n**1935 – Saar Plebiscite (January 13, 1935)**\nSaar Territory votes 90.73% to reunite with Germany. Transfer effective March 1, 1935. ~1,912 km² returned to the German Reich.\n\n**1935–1936 – Italy invades Ethiopia**\nItalian invasion begins October 3, 1935. **Addis Ababa** falls May 5, 1936. Italy proclaims the **Italian Empire (Africa Orientale Italiana/AOI)**, merging Ethiopia (~1,104,300 km²) with Eritrea and Italian Somaliland. Total AOI area: ~1,725,000 km². Ethiopia liberated 1941 by British and Ethiopian forces; Emperor Haile Selassie restored.\n\n**1936 – Anglo-Egyptian Treaty**\nBritain retains Suez Canal Zone garrison but recognizes fuller Egyptian sovereignty. British troops remain until 1956.\n\n**1937 – Ireland / Éire**\nNew constitution adopted July 1, 1937 (effective December 29, 1937). **Irish Free State** becomes **Éire/Ireland**; effectively a republic in all but name. ~70,273 km². (Formally declared Republic of Ireland April 18, 1949.)\n\n---\n\n## 2. WORLD WAR II TERRITORIAL CHANGES (1938–1945)\n\n### Pre-War Annexations (1938–1939)\n\n**1938 – Anschluss (March 12–13, 1938)**\nNazi Germany annexes **Austria**. Austria ceases to exist as an independent state. ~83,879 km² absorbed into the German Reich. Population: ~6.65 million. Re-established as independent 1945 (formally sovereign 1955).\n\n**1938 – Munich Agreement (September 30, 1938)**\nGermany annexes **Sudetenland** from Czechoslovakia. ~28,680 km². Population: ~3.5 million (predominantly ethnic German). Effective October 1–10, 1938.\n\n**1938 – First Vienna Award (November 2, 1938)**\nGermany and Italy arbitrate. **Hungary** receives **southern Slovakia and southern Subcarpathian Ruthenia** from Czechoslovakia. ~11,927 km². Population: ~1,041,000.\n\n**1938 – Poland and Czechoslovakia**\nOctober 1938: Poland seizes **Zaolzie/Trans-Olza** (Teschen/Český Těšín region) from Czechoslovakia. ~801 km². Population: ~227,000.\n\n**1939 – Dismemberment of Czechoslovakia (March 14–16, 1939)**\n- **Slovakia** declares independence (March 14, 1939, German puppet state). ~38,000 km².\n- **Bohemia and Moravia** occupied by Germany; proclaimed **Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia** (March 16, 1939). ~49,363 km².\n- **Subcarpathian Ruthenia/Carpatho-Ukraine** — declares independence March 15, 1939; immediately invaded and annexed by **Hungary** (March 15–18, 1939). ~12,800 km².\n\n**1939 – Hungary annexes Carpatho-Ukraine**\n(See above.) Hungary now controls all of Subcarpathian Ruthenia. ~12,800 km².\n\n**1939 – Memel/Klaipėda returned to Germany (March 22, 1939)**\nLithuania forced to cede Memel Territory to Germany. ~2,848 km².\n\n**1939 – Italy annexes Albania (April 7, 1939)**\nItalian invasion; personal union under Italian crown. ~28,748 km².\n\n**1939 – Hatay/Alexandretta (June 29, 1939)**\nFrance cedes the **Sanjak of Alexandretta (Hatay)** from French Mandate Syria to Turkey. ~4,855 km². Now Hatay Province of Turkey.\n\n**1939 – German invasion of Poland (September 1, 1939)**\n- Western Poland directly annexed into the Reich (Wartheland, Danzig-West Prussia, Silesia, etc.). ~91,974 km².\n- **Free City of Danzig** annexed by Germany (September 1, 1939). ~1,966 km².\n- Central Poland becomes **General Government** (German-occupied territory). ~95,742 km².\n\n**1939 – Soviet invasion of Eastern Poland (September 17, 1939)**\nUSSR occupies **Eastern Poland (Kresy)**: Western Belarus and Western Ukraine. ~201,015 km² (approximately). Formally annexed into the Ukrainian SSR and Byelorussian SSR following staged referenda (October–November 1939).\n\n### Soviet Expansions (1939–1940)\n\n**1939–1940 – Winter War / Treaty of Moscow (March 12, 1940)**\nFinland cedes to the USSR:\n- **Karelian Isthmus** including Vyborg/Viipuri (~)\n- **Ladoga Karelia** (north of Lake Ladoga)\n- **Salla area** (partial)\n- **Rybachy Peninsula** (Kalastajansaarento)\n- **Hanko Peninsula** — leased as naval base for 30 years\nTotal ceded: ~40,000 km² (about 11% of Finland's territory). Population displaced: ~422,000 Finns (essentially entire population evacuated).\n\n**1940 – Soviet annexation of the Baltic States**\n- **Lithuania**: Soviet ultimatum June 14, 1940. Occupied June 15, 1940. Proclaimed Lithuanian SSR, formally annexed August 3, 1940. ~65,300 km². (Vilnius region transferred from Poland/USSR to Lithuania October 1939, ~6,880 km².)\n- **Latvia**: Soviet ultimatum June 16, 1940. Occupied June 17, 1940. Proclaimed Latvian SSR, formally annexed August 5, 1940. ~64,589 km².\n- **Estonia**: Soviet ultimatum June 16, 1940. Occupied June 17, 1940. Proclaimed Estonian SSR, formally annexed August 6, 1940. ~45,228 km².\n\n**1940 – Soviet annexation of Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina (June 28, 1940)**\nUSSR issues ultimatum to Romania. Romania cedes:\n- **Bessarabia** → mostly incorporated into Moldavian SSR (~33,843 km²), southern part (Budjak) and northern part to Ukrainian SSR\n- **Northern Bukovina** → Ukrainian SSR (~6,000 km²)\n- **Hertsa region** → Ukrainian SSR (~300 km²)\nTotal: ~50,762 km². Moldavian SSR (previously Moldavian ASSR on east bank of Dniester) reconstituted to include most of Bessarabia. ~33,843 km² total for Moldavian SSR.\n\n**1940 – Second Vienna Award (August 30, 1940)**\nGermany and Italy arbitrate. **Hungary** receives **Northern Transylvania** from Romania. ~43,492 km². Population: ~2.6 million. (Returned to Romania in 1945.)\n\n**1940 – Treaty of Craiova (September 7, 1940)**\nRomania cedes **Southern Dobruja (Cadrilater)** to **Bulgaria**. ~7,695 km². Population: ~370,000. (This transfer was permanent; confirmed at Paris 1947.)\n\n### Wartime Occupations and Puppet States\n\n**1940 – Japanese occupation of French Indochina**\nJapan occupies northern French Indochina (September 1940), then southern Indochina (July 1941). French colonial administration continues under Japanese supervision until March 1945 coup.\n\n**1940–1941 – Italian/German campaigns**\n- Italy invades **Greece** (October 1940, from Albania) — fails; Germany conquers Greece April 1941\n- Germany conquers **Yugoslavia** (April 1941): dismembered into:\n  - **Independent State of Croatia (NDH)** — puppet state (~102,725 km², including Bosnia and Herzegovina)\n  - **Serbia** — German military administration (~51,100 km²)\n  - Parts annexed by Italy (Dalmatia, Montenegro as protectorate, Kosovo to Italian Albania, ~8,500 km²)\n  - Parts annexed by Hungary (Bačka, Baranja, Prekmurje, Međimurje, ~11,601 km²)\n  - Parts annexed by Bulgaria (Vardar Macedonia, southeastern Serbia, ~28,000 km²)\n  - Parts annexed by Germany (northern Slovenia, ~9,620 km²)\n  - Italy annexes southern Slovenia (~4,550 km², Province of Ljubljana)\n\n**1941 – Germany invades USSR (June 22, 1941)**\nOccupies Baltic states, Belarus, Ukraine, western Russia. Not a permanent border change but creates wartime occupation zones.\n\n**1941 – Japan expands**\n- **Pearl Harbor** (December 7, 1941) brings USA into war\n- Japan conquers: Philippines, Malaya, Singapore, Dutch East Indies, Burma, Hong Kong, Wake Island, Guam, much of Pacific Islands (1941–1942)\n- **Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere** at maximum extent (mid-1942): ~7,400,000 km²\n\n**1941 – Continuation War (Finland, June 1941–September 1944)**\nFinland re-occupies lost Karelian territories (1941) but loses them again at armistice (September 19, 1944). Finland also cedes **Petsamo/Pechenga** (~10,470 km²) to USSR (confirmed Paris 1947). Porkkala Peninsula leased to USSR (returned 1956).\n\n**1943 – Italy surrenders (September 8, 1943)**\nItaly switches sides. Germany occupies northern and central Italy; Italian Social Republic (puppet) established at Salò.\n\n### End of WWII (1945)\n\n**1945 – Germany surrenders (May 8, 1945)**\nGermany divided into four occupation zones:\n- **American Zone** (Bavaria, Hesse, northern Württemberg-Baden, Bremen enclave)\n- **British Zone** (Northwest Germany: Schleswig-Holstein, Lower Saxony, North Rhine-Westphalia, Hamburg)\n- **French Zone** (Rhineland-Palatinate, southern Baden, southern Württemberg, Saarland)\n- **Soviet Zone** (Thuringia, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt, Brandenburg, Mecklenburg)\n- **Berlin** — divided into four sectors (American, British, French, Soviet)\nTotal Germany (1937 borders): ~633,786 km²; post-war zones on territory west of Oder-Neisse: ~357,022 km²\n\n**1945 – Austrian independence restored**\nAlso divided into four occupation zones. Treated as a liberated (not enemy) state. ~83,879 km². Full sovereignty restored by Austrian State Treaty, May 15, 1955.\n\n**1945 – Poland's borders shift westward**\n- **Eastern Poland** (Kresy) — formally retained by USSR (Western Belarus and Western Ukraine, as occupied since 1939). ~178,000 km² lost.\n- **Western/northern gains from Germany**: Poland receives **Silesia, Pomerania, southern East Prussia, eastern Brandenburg** east of the Oder-Neisse Line. ~112,000 km² gained (the \"Recovered Territories\").\n- Net Poland: ~312,685 km² (vs. ~389,720 km² pre-war). Shifted approximately 200 km westward.\n\n**1945 – USSR acquires Königsberg/Kaliningrad**\nNorthern **East Prussia** (including Königsberg) annexed by USSR. ~15,100 km². Becomes **Kaliningrad Oblast** of the RSFSR. German population expelled.\n\n**1945 – Manchukuo dissolved**\nJapanese puppet state ceases to exist. Territory returned to China (Republic of China). ~1,192,000 km².\n\n**1945 – Japanese Empire dismantled**\n- **Korea** liberated from Japanese rule (August 15, 1945); divided at 38th parallel into Soviet (north) and American (south) zones\n- **Taiwan (Formosa)** returned to Republic of China. ~36,000 km²\n- **Sakhalin (southern)** and **Kuril Islands** occupied by USSR. ~46,000 km² total (South Sakhalin ~36,090 km² + Kurils ~10,503 km²)\n- **Pacific Islands (Mandate)**: Mariana, Caroline, Marshall Islands → US Trust Territory (formalized 1947). ~1,779 km² land area.\n- **Okinawa/Ryukyu Islands** → US administration (returned to Japan 1972). ~2,271 km².\n\n**1945 – Tannu Tuva formally annexed by USSR (October 11, 1944)**\nThe **Tuvan People's Republic** (nominally independent since 1921, Soviet satellite) is annexed as the Tuvan Autonomous Oblast of the RSFSR. ~170,500 km².\n\n**1945 – Subcarpathian Ruthenia/Carpatho-Ukraine ceded to USSR**\nCzechoslovakia cedes **Subcarpathian Ruthenia** to the USSR (treaty June 29, 1945). ~12,800 km². Incorporated into Ukrainian SSR (Transcarpathian Oblast).\n\n---\n\n## 3. POST-WWII SETTLEMENTS (1945–1955)\n\n### Europe\n\n**1946 – Italian peace settlement / Paris Peace Treaties (February 10, 1947)**\n- Italy cedes **Istria** (most of) and **Fiume/Rijeka** to Yugoslavia. ~7,000 km².\n- Italy cedes **Zara/Zadar** to Yugoslavia\n- **Dodecanese Islands** ceded by Italy to **Greece** (effective March 31, 1947). ~2,714 km².\n- **Free Territory of Trieste** created (never fully implemented). ~738 km². Divided into:\n  - Zone A (including Trieste city) — Anglo-American administration (~223 km²)\n  - Zone B — Yugoslav administration (~515 km²)\n  - Resolved 1954 (Treaty of London, formalized Treaty of Osimo 1975): Zone A → Italy, Zone B → Yugoslavia.\n- Italy cedes small border areas to France (~700 km²: Tende/Tenda, La Brigue/Briga, Mont Cenis plateau)\n- Italian colonies: Libya, Eritrea, Italian Somaliland stripped (independence/UN trusteeship later)\n\n**1947 – Hungarian peace treaty (Paris)**\nHungary returns to essentially Trianon borders. Northern Transylvania back to Romania. Three additional villages near Bratislava (~63 km²) ceded to Czechoslovakia.\n\n**1947 – Romanian peace treaty (Paris)**\nConfirms: Northern Transylvania back from Hungary. Bessarabia and Northern Bukovina remain with USSR. Southern Dobruja remains with Bulgaria.\n\n**1947 – Bulgarian peace treaty (Paris)**\nConfirms Bulgarian retention of **Southern Dobruja** from Romania (~7,695 km²). Bulgaria returns Vardar Macedonia and Greek territories occupied during war.\n\n**1947 – Finnish peace treaty (Paris)**\nConfirms Soviet retention of:\n- Karelian Isthmus and Ladoga Karelia (~)\n- Salla area\n- **Petsamo/Pechenga** (~10,470 km²) — Finland loses Arctic access permanently\n- Porkkala Peninsula leased (returned 1956)\nTotal Finnish losses: ~45,000 km² (about 12% of pre-war territory)\n\n**1948 – Korea split formalized**\n- **Republic of Korea (South Korea)** proclaimed August 15, 1948. ~100,210 km².\n- **Democratic People's Republic of Korea (North Korea)** proclaimed September 9, 1948. ~120,538 km².\n- Korean War (1950–1953) causes massive destruction but final armistice line (July 27, 1953) roughly follows 38th parallel with minor variations; South Korea gains ~3,900 km² compared to the original 38th parallel division, North Korea gains ~3,100 km².\n\n**1949 – Two German states**\n- **Federal Republic of Germany (West Germany/BRD)** proclaimed May 23, 1949. American, British, French zones. ~248,577 km² (including West Berlin, ~480 km²).\n- **German Democratic Republic (East Germany/DDR)** proclaimed October 7, 1949. Soviet zone. ~108,333 km² (including East Berlin, ~403 km²).\n- **Saarland** — French protectorate (1947); autonomous with economic union with France. ~2,569 km². Plebiscite October 23, 1955, rejects European statute. Joined West Germany January 1, 1957 (\"Kleine Wiedervereinigung\").\n\n### Asia\n\n**1947 – Indian Partition (August 14–15, 1947)**\nBritish India partitioned at independence:\n- **India (Dominion of India)** proclaimed August 15, 1947. ~3,287,263 km² (including princely states that acceded).\n- **Pakistan (Dominion of Pakistan)** proclaimed August 14, 1947. Comprised two non-contiguous parts:\n  - **West Pakistan** (~796,095 km²) — Punjab (west), Sindh, Balochistan, NWFP\n  - **East Pakistan** (~147,570 km²) — Eastern Bengal (becomes Bangladesh 1971)\n- **Princely states** (562 states): most accede to India or Pakistan. Key disputes:\n  - **Jammu and Kashmir** — accedes to India (October 26, 1947) amid tribal invasion. First Kashmir War (1947–1948). Ceasefire Line (January 1, 1949) divides territory: India controls ~101,338 km² (Jammu, Kashmir Valley, Ladakh); Pakistan controls ~78,114 km² (\"Azad Kashmir\" + Gilgit-Baltistan)\n  - **Hyderabad** — Indian military action (Operation Polo, September 1948) annexes (~82,698 km²)\n  - **Junagadh** — accedes to Pakistan but Indian occupation (November 1947, plebiscite February 1948) incorporates into India\n  - **Goa, Daman, Diu** — remain Portuguese (until Indian annexation 1961)\n  - **Pondicherry** — remains French (until transfer 1954, formal cession 1962)\n\n**1947 – Burma (Myanmar) independence (January 4, 1948)**\nFrom British rule. ~676,578 km².\n\n**1947 – Ceylon (Sri Lanka) independence (February 4, 1948)**\nFrom British rule. ~65,610 km². (Renamed Sri Lanka 1972.)\n\n**1948 – State of Israel proclaimed (May 14, 1948)**\nBritish Mandate of Palestine ends. UN Partition Plan (Resolution 181, November 1947) proposed Jewish state (~14,100 km²) and Arab state (~11,500 km²) with Jerusalem as international zone (~176 km²). Arab-Israeli War (1948–1949):\n- **Israel** at armistice: ~20,770 km² (significantly more than partition plan)\n- **West Bank** (including East Jerusalem): occupied/annexed by **Jordan** (~5,878 km²)\n- **Gaza Strip**: occupied by **Egypt** (~365 km²)\n- No independent Palestinian Arab state created\n\n**1949 – People's Republic of China (October 1, 1949)**\nChinese Communist Party victory in civil war. **PRC** controls mainland China (~9,596,961 km²).\n**Republic of China (Taiwan)** government retreats to Taiwan, Penghu, Kinmen, Matsu (~36,193 km²).\n\n**1949 – Indonesia independence (December 27, 1949)**\nNetherlands formally transfers sovereignty. **Republic of the United States of Indonesia** (~1,904,569 km²), quickly replaced by unitary Republic of Indonesia (August 17, 1950). **West New Guinea/Papua** remains Dutch (transferred to Indonesia 1963 via UN).\n\n**1949 – Transjordan becomes Jordan**\nRenamed **Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan** (April 1949). Formally annexes **West Bank** (April 1950). Total area: ~89,342 km² (East Bank) + ~5,878 km² (West Bank) = ~95,220 km².\n\n**1951 – Libya independence (December 24, 1951)**\nFormer Italian colony. United Kingdom of Libya (federation of Tripolitania, Cyrenaica, Fezzan). ~1,759,540 km².\n\n---\n\n## 4. DECOLONIZATION: AFRICA (1951–1968)\n\n### North Africa\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date of Independence |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|---------------------|\n| 1951 | **Libya** | Italy (UN-administered) | 1,759,540 | December 24, 1951 |\n| 1956 | **Sudan** | Anglo-Egyptian Condominium | 2,505,813 | January 1, 1956 |\n| 1956 | **Morocco** | France (+ Spanish zones) | 446,550 | March 2, 1956 (French zone); April 7, 1956 (Spanish zone ceded except enclaves) |\n| 1956 | **Tunisia** | France | 163,610 | March 20, 1956 |\n\n### Sub-Saharan Africa — Chronological\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date of Independence |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|---------------------|\n| 1957 | **Ghana** | Britain (Gold Coast + British Togoland) | 238,533 | March 6, 1957 |\n| 1958 | **Guinea** | France | 245,857 | October 2, 1958 |\n| **1960 — \"Year of Africa\" (17 countries):** | | | | |\n| 1960 | **Cameroon** | France (UN Trust) | 475,442 | January 1, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Senegal** | France (Mali Federation, then independent) | 196,722 | April 4, 1960 (from Mali Federation; Mali Federation independent June 20, 1960; Senegal secedes August 20, 1960) |\n| 1960 | **Togo** | France (UN Trust) | 56,785 | April 27, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Mali** | France (as Sudanese Republic in Mali Federation) | 1,240,192 | June 20, 1960 (Mali Federation); September 22, 1960 (Republic of Mali after Senegal's secession) |\n| 1960 | **Madagascar** | France | 587,041 | June 26, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Democratic Republic of the Congo** | Belgium (Belgian Congo) | 2,344,858 | June 30, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Somalia** | Britain (Somaliland) + Italy (UN Trust, Italian Somaliland) | 637,657 | June 26, 1960 (British Somaliland); July 1, 1960 (merger with Italian Somaliland = Somali Republic) |\n| 1960 | **Benin** (Dahomey) | France | 112,622 | August 1, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Niger** | France | 1,267,000 | August 3, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Burkina Faso** (Upper Volta) | France | 274,200 | August 5, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Ivory Coast (Côte d'Ivoire)** | France | 322,463 | August 7, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Chad** | France | 1,284,000 | August 11, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Central African Republic** | France | 622,984 | August 13, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Republic of the Congo** (Congo-Brazzaville) | France | 342,000 | August 15, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Gabon** | France | 267,668 | August 17, 1960 |\n| 1960 | **Mauritania** | France | 1,030,700 | November 28, 1960 |\n\n### Post-1960 Wave\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date of Independence |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|---------------------|\n| 1961 | **Sierra Leone** | Britain | 71,740 | April 27, 1961 |\n| 1961 | **Cameroon** (expanded) | Merged with Southern British Cameroons | +42,000 (to existing) | October 1, 1961 (Southern Cameroons joins Cameroon; Northern Cameroons joins Nigeria June 1, 1961) |\n| 1961 | **Tanganyika** | Britain (UN Trust) | 945,087 | December 9, 1961 |\n| 1962 | **Algeria** | France | 2,381,741 | July 5, 1962 (following Evian Accords; referendum) |\n| 1962 | **Rwanda** | Belgium (UN Trust) | 26,338 | July 1, 1962 |\n| 1962 | **Burundi** | Belgium (UN Trust) | 27,834 | July 1, 1962 |\n| 1962 | **Uganda** | Britain | 241,038 | October 9, 1962 |\n| 1963 | **Kenya** | Britain | 580,367 | December 12, 1963 |\n| 1963 | **Zanzibar** | Britain | 2,461 | December 10, 1963 (revolution January 12, 1964; merged with Tanganyika to form **Tanzania** April 26, 1964) |\n| 1964 | **Tanzania** | Union of Tanganyika + Zanzibar | 947,303 | April 26, 1964 |\n| 1964 | **Malawi** | Britain (Nyasaland) | 118,484 | July 6, 1964 |\n| 1964 | **Zambia** | Britain (Northern Rhodesia) | 752,612 | October 24, 1964 |\n| 1965 | **The Gambia** | Britain | 11,295 | February 18, 1965 |\n| 1966 | **Botswana** | Britain (Bechuanaland) | 581,730 | September 30, 1966 |\n| 1966 | **Lesotho** | Britain (Basutoland) | 30,355 | October 4, 1966 |\n| 1968 | **Mauritius** | Britain | 2,040 | March 12, 1968 |\n| 1968 | **Swaziland** (now Eswatini) | Britain | 17,364 | September 6, 1968 |\n| 1968 | **Equatorial Guinea** | Spain | 28,051 | October 12, 1968 |\n\n### Later African Independence\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date of Independence |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|---------------------|\n| 1974 | **Guinea-Bissau** | Portugal | 36,125 | September 10, 1974 (declared 1973; recognized 1974) |\n| 1975 | **Mozambique** | Portugal | 801,590 | June 25, 1975 |\n| 1975 | **Cape Verde** | Portugal | 4,033 | July 5, 1975 |\n| 1975 | **São Tomé and Príncipe** | Portugal | 964 | July 12, 1975 |\n| 1975 | **Comoros** | France | 1,862 | July 6, 1975 (Mayotte remains French) |\n| 1975 | **Angola** | Portugal | 1,246,700 | November 11, 1975 |\n| 1976 | **Seychelles** | Britain | 455 | June 29, 1976 |\n| 1977 | **Djibouti** | France (Afars and Issas) | 23,200 | June 27, 1977 |\n| 1980 | **Zimbabwe** | Britain (Rhodesia UDI 1965) | 390,757 | April 18, 1980 |\n| 1990 | **Namibia** | South Africa (UN mandate) | 824,292 | March 21, 1990 |\n\n---\n\n## 5. DECOLONIZATION: ASIA AND MIDDLE EAST\n\n### Southeast Asia\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|------|\n| 1946 | **Philippines** | United States | 300,000 | July 4, 1946 |\n| 1948 | **Burma (Myanmar)** | Britain | 676,578 | January 4, 1948 |\n| 1949 | **Indonesia** | Netherlands | 1,904,569 | December 27, 1949 |\n| 1953 | **Cambodia** | France | 181,035 | November 9, 1953 |\n| 1954 | **Laos** | France | 236,800 | Fully independent July 19, 1949; Geneva Accords confirm 1954 |\n| 1954 | **Vietnam split** | France (Geneva Accords July 21, 1954) | 331,212 total | **North Vietnam (DRV)**: ~157,880 km² north of 17th parallel; **South Vietnam (RVN)**: ~173,809 km² south of 17th parallel |\n| 1957 | **Malaya (Federation of Malaya)** | Britain | 131,598 | August 31, 1957 |\n| 1963 | **Malaysia** (formation) | Merger of Malaya, Singapore, Sabah (North Borneo), Sarawak | 330,803 | September 16, 1963 |\n| 1965 | **Singapore** separation from Malaysia | — | 719 | August 9, 1965 |\n| 1984 | **Brunei** | Britain | 5,765 | January 1, 1984 |\n\n### South Asia\n\n| Year | Country | Notes | Area (km²) | Date |\n|------|---------|-------|------------|------|\n| 1947 | **India** | British India partition | 3,287,263 | August 15, 1947 |\n| 1947 | **Pakistan** | British India partition | 943,665 (W+E) | August 14, 1947 |\n| 1948 | **Ceylon (Sri Lanka)** | Britain | 65,610 | February 4, 1948 |\n| 1954 | **Pondicherry/French India** | France cedes to India (de facto 1954, de jure 1962) | 492 | November 1, 1954 |\n| 1961 | **Goa, Daman, Diu** | India annexes from Portugal (Operation Vijay, December 1961) | 3,702 | December 19, 1961 |\n| 1965 | **Maldives** | Britain | 298 | July 26, 1965 |\n| 1971 | **Bangladesh** | Secedes from Pakistan (East Pakistan) after war | 147,570 | March 26, 1971 (declared); December 16, 1971 (Pakistani surrender) |\n| 1975 | **Sikkim** | Annexed by India (referendum April 1975) | 7,096 | May 16, 1975 |\n\n### Middle East\n\n| Year | Country | Notes | Area (km²) | Date |\n|------|---------|-------|------------|------|\n| 1932 | **Iraq** | British Mandate ends | 435,052 | October 3, 1932 |\n| 1943 | **Lebanon** | French Mandate ends (de facto) | 10,452 | November 22, 1943 |\n| 1946 | **Syria** | French Mandate ends | 185,180 | April 17, 1946 |\n| 1946 | **Jordan** (Transjordan) | British Mandate ends | 89,342 | May 25, 1946 |\n| 1948 | **Israel** | British Mandate of Palestine ends | 20,770 (at armistice) | May 14, 1948 |\n| 1961 | **Kuwait** | Britain | 17,818 | June 19, 1961 |\n| 1967 | **South Yemen (People's Republic of South Yemen)** | Britain (Aden Colony/Protectorate) | 332,968 | November 30, 1967 |\n| 1971 | **Bahrain** | Britain | 765 | August 15, 1971 |\n| 1971 | **Qatar** | Britain | 11,586 | September 3, 1971 |\n| 1971 | **United Arab Emirates** | Britain (Trucial States) | 83,600 | December 2, 1971 (federation of Abu Dhabi, Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm al-Quwain, Fujairah; Ras al-Khaimah joins February 10, 1972) |\n| 1971 | **Oman** (modernized) | Never formally colonized, but British-influenced | 309,500 | Already independent; treaty revisions 1971 |\n\n### East Asia\n\n| Year | Event | Details |\n|------|-------|---------|\n| 1945 | **Korea divided** | 38th parallel. US zone (south), Soviet zone (north) |\n| 1948 | **Two Koreas** | ROK (South, August 15) and DPRK (North, September 9) |\n| 1949 | **PRC established** | Mainland China. ROC retreats to Taiwan. |\n| 1952 | **Japan regains sovereignty** | San Francisco Treaty (September 8, 1951; effective April 28, 1952). Japan renounces claims to Korea, Taiwan, Sakhalin, Kurils, Pacific mandate islands. Japan: ~377,975 km² (excluding Okinawa, under US control until 1972). |\n| 1972 | **Okinawa returned to Japan** | May 15, 1972. ~2,271 km². |\n\n---\n\n## 6. DECOLONIZATION: AMERICAS AND OCEANIA\n\n### Caribbean and Americas\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|------|\n| 1962 | **Jamaica** | Britain | 10,991 | August 6, 1962 |\n| 1962 | **Trinidad and Tobago** | Britain | 5,131 | August 31, 1962 |\n| 1966 | **Guyana** | Britain | 214,969 | May 26, 1966 |\n| 1966 | **Barbados** | Britain | 430 | November 30, 1966 |\n| 1973 | **The Bahamas** | Britain | 13,878 | July 10, 1973 |\n| 1974 | **Grenada** | Britain | 344 | February 7, 1974 |\n| 1975 | **Suriname** | Netherlands | 163,820 | November 25, 1975 |\n| 1978 | **Dominica** | Britain | 751 | November 3, 1978 |\n| 1979 | **Saint Lucia** | Britain | 616 | February 22, 1979 |\n| 1979 | **Saint Vincent and the Grenadines** | Britain | 389 | October 27, 1979 |\n| 1981 | **Belize** | Britain (British Honduras) | 22,966 | September 21, 1981 |\n| 1983 | **Saint Kitts and Nevis** | Britain | 261 | September 19, 1983 |\n\n### Oceania / Pacific\n\n| Year | Country | Former Colonial Power | Area (km²) | Date |\n|------|---------|----------------------|------------|------|\n| 1962 | **Samoa** (Western Samoa) | New Zealand (UN Trust) | 2,842 | January 1, 1962 |\n| 1968 | **Nauru** | Australia/NZ/UK (UN Trust) | 21 | January 31, 1968 |\n| 1970 | **Fiji** | Britain | 18,274 | October 10, 1970 |\n| 1970 | **Tonga** | Britain (protected state) | 747 | June 4, 1970 |\n| 1975 | **Papua New Guinea** | Australia (UN Trust) | 462,840 | September 16, 1975 |\n| 1978 | **Solomon Islands** | Britain | 28,896 | July 7, 1978 |\n| 1978 | **Tuvalu** | Britain (separated from Gilbert Islands) | 26 | October 1, 1978 |\n| 1979 | **Kiribati** | Britain (Gilbert Islands) | 811 | July 12, 1979 |\n| 1980 | **Vanuatu** | Britain/France (New Hebrides condominium) | 12,189 | July 30, 1980 |\n| 1986 | **Marshall Islands** | US (Compact of Free Association) | 181 | October 21, 1986 |\n| 1986 | **Federated States of Micronesia** | US (Compact of Free Association) | 702 | November 3, 1986 |\n| 1994 | **Palau** | US (UN Trust, last) | 459 | October 1, 1994 |\n\n---\n\n## 7. COLD WAR ERA TERRITORIAL CHANGES (1950–1991)\n\n### Arab-Israeli Conflicts\n\n**1956 – Suez Crisis (October 29 – November 7, 1956)**\nIsrael, Britain, and France attack Egypt. Israel briefly occupies **Sinai Peninsula** (~60,000 km²). International pressure forces withdrawal by March 1957. No permanent border changes. UN Emergency Force (UNEF) deployed.\n\n**1967 – Six-Day War (June 5–10, 1967)**\nIsrael captures:\n- **Sinai Peninsula** from Egypt (~60,000 km²)\n- **Gaza Strip** from Egyptian control (~365 km²)\n- **West Bank** (including East Jerusalem) from Jordan (~5,878 km²)\n- **Golan Heights** from Syria (~1,800 km² of occupied territory; ~1,150 km² formally annexed by Israel 1981)\nTotal occupied: ~68,000 km². Israel's effective control expands from ~20,770 km² to ~89,000 km².\n\n**1967 – East Jerusalem annexed by Israel (June 27–28, 1967)**\nIsrael extends municipal boundaries and law to East Jerusalem. ~70 km². Not internationally recognized.\n\n**1973 – Yom Kippur War (October 6–25, 1973)**\nEgypt and Syria attack Israel. No permanent border changes at armistice, but:\n- Sinai disengagement agreements (1974, 1975) create buffer zones\n- Golan disengagement agreement (1974) — UNDOF zone established\n\n**1978 – Camp David Accords (September 17, 1978)**\nFramework for Egypt-Israel peace. Treaty signed March 26, 1979.\n\n**1979–1982 – Sinai returned to Egypt**\nPhased withdrawal:\n- Phase 1: Al-Arish and eastern Sinai returned (1979–1980)\n- Final withdrawal: April 25, 1982. Entire **Sinai Peninsula** (~60,000 km²) returned to Egypt.\n- Exception: **Taba** (~1 km²) — disputed; returned to Egypt March 15, 1989 after international arbitration.\n\n### South Asian Conflicts\n\n**1962 – Sino-Indian War (October 20 – November 21, 1962)**\nChina defeats India in border war:\n- **Aksai Chin** (~37,244 km²): China retains control (had occupied since 1950s); India still claims\n- **Arunachal Pradesh** (then NEFA, ~83,743 km²): China invades then withdraws. India retains control; China still claims (\"South Tibet\")\n- Net result: status quo ante with China holding Aksai Chin\n\n**1965 – Second Kashmir War (August–September 1965)**\nIndia-Pakistan war. Tashkent Declaration (January 1966). No significant border changes; return to pre-war positions.\n\n**1971 – Bangladesh Liberation War**\nIndia intervenes in East Pakistan civil war. **East Pakistan** becomes **Bangladesh** (December 16, 1971). ~147,570 km². Pakistan loses ~55% of its population and ~15% of its territory.\n\n### Southeast Asia\n\n**1954 – Vietnam divided at 17th parallel**\n(See above, Section 5.)\n\n**1975 – Vietnam reunified (April 30, 1975)**\nFall of Saigon. North Vietnam conquers South Vietnam. **Socialist Republic of Vietnam** proclaimed July 2, 1976. ~331,212 km². South Vietnam ceases to exist.\n\n**1975 – Cambodia falls to Khmer Rouge (April 17, 1975)**\nDemocratic Kampuchea established. ~181,035 km².\n\n**1975 – Laos — Pathet Lao victory (December 2, 1975)**\nLao People's Democratic Republic proclaimed. Monarchy abolished. ~236,800 km².\n\n**1976 – Indonesia annexes East Timor**\nPortugal withdraws from **East Timor** (1975). FRETILIN declares independence November 28, 1975. Indonesia invades December 7, 1975. Formally annexed as 27th province July 17, 1976. ~14,874 km². (Independence restored May 20, 2002, after UN administration.)\n\n**1979 – Sino-Vietnamese War (February 17 – March 16, 1979)**\nChina invades northern Vietnam, then withdraws. No permanent border changes. Border skirmishes continue until 1991.\n\n### Western Sahara Crisis\n\n**1975 – Spanish Sahara decolonization**\nSpain withdraws from **Spanish Sahara** (~266,000 km²). Madrid Accords (November 14, 1975) divide territory between:\n- **Morocco** (northern 2/3, ~)\n- **Mauritania** (southern 1/3, ~)\n**Sahrawi Arab Democratic Republic (SADR)** proclaimed February 27, 1976 by Polisario Front.\n\n**1979 – Mauritania withdraws from Western Sahara (August 5, 1979)**\nMauritania renounces claims. **Morocco** occupies the entire territory (~266,000 km²). Conflict continues; Morocco builds sand wall/berm (1980–1987) dividing territory into Moroccan-controlled west and Polisario-held east. Ceasefire 1991. Status remains unresolved.\n\n### Cyprus\n\n**1960 – Cyprus independence (August 16, 1960)**\nFrom Britain. ~9,251 km². British retain sovereign base areas at Akrotiri and Dhekelia (~254 km²).\n\n**1974 – Turkish invasion of Cyprus (July 20, 1974)**\nTurkey invades northern Cyprus. Ceasefire establishes:\n- **Northern Cyprus** under Turkish control (~3,355 km², ~36% of island). Declares itself \"Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus\" (November 15, 1983; recognized only by Turkey).\n- **Republic of Cyprus** controls south (~5,896 km²)\n- UN buffer zone established\n\n### Africa — Post-Colonial Border Changes\n\n**1961 – Cameroon expansion**\nBritish Southern Cameroons joins Republic of Cameroon (October 1, 1961). Northern Cameroons joins Nigeria (June 1, 1961).\n\n**1963 – West New Guinea/Papua transferred to Indonesia**\nNetherlands transfers via UN administration (UNTEA, October 1, 1962 – May 1, 1963). \"Act of Free Choice\" (1969) — controversial vote endorses Indonesian sovereignty. ~421,981 km².\n\n**1964 – Zanzibar merges with Tanganyika → Tanzania** (April 26, 1964)\n(See Section 4.)\n\n**1975 – Comoros independence**\nThree islands (Grande Comore, Mohéli, Anjouan) become independent. **Mayotte** votes to remain French. ~1,862 km² independent; ~374 km² remains French.\n\n**1976 – French Territory of the Afars and Issas → Djibouti (1977)**\nIndependence June 27, 1977. ~23,200 km².\n\n**1977 – Ogaden War (July 1977 – March 1978)**\nSomalia invades Ethiopian Ogaden region (~350,000 km²). Ethiopia, with Soviet and Cuban support, repels invasion. No border changes.\n\n### Other Changes\n\n**1958 – United Arab Republic (UAR)**\nEgypt and Syria merge (February 1, 1958). Syria secedes (September 28, 1961). Egypt continues using \"UAR\" name until 1971.\n\n**1961 – Iraq threatens Kuwait**\nIraq claims Kuwait upon its independence. British forces deter. No border change.\n\n**1965 – Rhodesia Unilateral Declaration of Independence (UDI)**\nNovember 11, 1965. White-minority government under Ian Smith. Not internationally recognized. ~390,757 km². Becomes Zimbabwe April 18, 1980.\n\n**1967 – Union of Egypt and Syria name changes**\nNot a territorial change per se but relevant for polity tracking: Egypt becomes \"Arab Republic of Egypt\" (1971).\n\n**1971 – Indo-Pakistani War / Bangladesh**\n(See above.) Additionally, Simla Agreement (July 2, 1972) converts Kashmir ceasefire line to **Line of Control (LoC)**; minor adjustments from 1949 line.\n\n**1974 – Ethiopian Revolution**\nDerg overthrows Haile Selassie. No territorial change but shifts polity from monarchy to socialist republic. Eritrean independence war intensifies.\n\n**1980 – Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988)**\nNo permanent border changes despite massive conflict. Shatt al-Arab waterway dispute returns to status quo ante.\n\n---\n\n## 8. DISSOLUTION OF THE SOVIET UNION (1991)\n\nThe USSR formally dissolved on December 26, 1991. Fifteen union republics became independent states. The process began in 1990 with declarations of sovereignty/independence:\n\n| # | New State | Declaration of Independence | Area (km²) | Capital |\n|---|-----------|---------------------------|------------|---------|\n| 1 | **Lithuania** | March 11, 1990 (first) | 65,300 | Vilnius |\n| 2 | **Georgia** | April 9, 1991 | 69,700 | Tbilisi |\n| 3 | **Estonia** | August 20, 1991 | 45,228 | Tallinn |\n| 4 | **Latvia** | August 21, 1991 | 64,589 | Riga |\n| 5 | **Ukraine** | August 24, 1991 | 603,550 | Kyiv |\n| 6 | **Belarus** | August 25, 1991 | 207,600 | Minsk |\n| 7 | **Moldova** | August 27, 1991 | 33,846 | Chișinău |\n| 8 | **Azerbaijan** | August 30, 1991 | 86,600 | Baku |\n| 9 | **Kyrgyzstan** | August 31, 1991 | 199,951 | Bishkek |\n| 10 | **Uzbekistan** | September 1, 1991 | 447,400 | Tashkent |\n| 11 | **Tajikistan** | September 9, 1991 | 143,100 | Dushanbe |\n| 12 | **Armenia** | September 21, 1991 | 29,743 | Yerevan |\n| 13 | **Turkmenistan** | October 27, 1991 | 488,100 | Ashgabat |\n| 14 | **Kazakhstan** | December 16, 1991 (last) | 2,724,900 | Alma-Ata (now Astana) |\n| 15 | **Russia (Russian Federation)** | Successor state to USSR | 17,098,242 | Moscow |\n\n**Total former USSR area: ~22,402,200 km²**\n\nKey associated events:\n- **Belavezha Accords** (December 8, 1991): leaders of Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus declare USSR dissolved; establish **Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS)**.\n- **Alma-Ata Protocol** (December 21, 1991): 11 of 15 republics (all except Baltic states and Georgia) join CIS.\n- **Gorbachev resigns** December 25, 1991. Soviet flag lowered over Kremlin December 25, 1991.\n- **USSR formally ceases to exist** December 26, 1991.\n\n### Territorial Disputes Arising from Soviet Dissolution\n\n- **Nagorno-Karabakh**: Armenian-majority enclave (~4,400 km²) within Azerbaijan. War 1988–1994; Armenian forces control region plus surrounding Azerbaijani territories.\n- **Transnistria**: Russian/Ukrainian-majority strip (~4,163 km²) within Moldova. Breakaway 1990; war 1992; de facto independent, unrecognized.\n- **Abkhazia** and **South Ossetia**: Breakaway regions of Georgia. Wars 1991–1993 (Abkhazia, ~8,660 km²) and 1991–1992 (South Ossetia, ~3,900 km²).\n- **Chechnya**: Declares independence 1991 from Russia. Wars 1994–1996 and 1999–2009. Remains within Russian Federation.\n- **Crimea**: Transferred from RSFSR to Ukrainian SSR in 1954 (~27,000 km²). Autonomous Republic within independent Ukraine 1991.\n\n---\n\n## ADDITIONAL POLITY-RELEVANT EVENTS (Supplementary)\n\n### German Reunification (1990)\n\n**1990 – German reunification (October 3, 1990)**\n**GDR (East Germany)** ceases to exist. Five reconstituted Länder (Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt, Thuringia) plus East Berlin accede to the Federal Republic of Germany under Article 23 of the Basic Law. **Two-Plus-Four Treaty** (September 12, 1990) — final settlement: Germany confirms Oder-Neisse border with Poland; renounces claims to pre-1945 eastern territories. Unified Germany: ~357,022 km².\n\n### Ethiopian/Eritrean Situation\n\n**1952 – Eritrea federated with Ethiopia** (UN resolution). ~117,600 km².\n**1962 – Ethiopia annexes Eritrea** as a province (dissolves federation).\n**1991 – Eritrean People's Liberation Front captures Asmara** (May 24, 1991). De facto independence. Formal independence via referendum: **April 27, 1993** (post-1991 but rooted in this period).\n\n### Yemen Unification\n\n**1990 – Yemen unification (May 22, 1990)**\n**Yemen Arab Republic (North Yemen)** (~195,000 km²) and **People's Democratic Republic of Yemen (South Yemen)** (~332,968 km²) merge into the **Republic of Yemen** (~527,968 km²).\n\n---\n\n## Summary Statistics\n\n- **Total new sovereign states created, 1920–1991**: approximately 130+\n- **Largest single territorial transfer**: Polish border shift 1945 (~178,000 km² lost east, ~112,000 km² gained west)\n- **Largest new state by area**: Kazakhstan (2,724,900 km²)\n- **Smallest new state by area**: Nauru (21 km²)\n- **Year with most new states**: 1960 (17 African states in a single year)\n- **Largest empire dissolved**: British Empire (decolonization spanning 1947–1984)\n- **Last major dissolution in period**: USSR (1991, 15 states)\n\n---\n\nThis catalog is designed to support polity-tracking by providing the precise year, territorial definition, area, and predecessor/successor relationships for each political entity. For any given year in the 1920–1991 range, the active set of polities can be reconstructed by applying these changes sequentially to the post-WWI baseline."}],"stop_reason":"end_turn","stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3,"cache_creation_input_tokens":1183,"cache_read_input_tokens":7571,"output_tokens":15979,"server_tool_use":{"web_search_requests":0,"web_fetch_requests":0},"service_tier":"standard","cache_creation":{"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":1183},"inference_geo":"","iterations":[],"speed":"standard"}},"requestId":"req_011CZNX4H5qTk6CeGEyeVniv","type":"assistant","uuid":"c2e8b598-c190-4564-b41d-2f07c1d6db64","timestamp":"2026-03-24T20:20:09.326Z","userType":"external","entrypoint":"cli","cwd":"/home/catalin/WHEP-refactor-polities","sessionId":"b5ea2b37-3948-44f5-9e01-2ef45e84c64c","version":"2.1.81","gitBranch":"lbm364dl/refactor-polities","slug":"sequential-inventing-treehouse"}
